```
import re
resentencesp = re.compile('([﹒﹔﹖﹗．；。！？]["’”」』]{0,2}|：(?=["‘“「『]{1,2}|$))')
def splitsentence(sentence):
    '''Split a piece of Chinese into sentences.'''
    # s = ''.join((chr(ord(ch)+0xFEE0) if ch in halfwidth else ch) for ch in sentence)
    s = sentence
    slist = []
    for i in resentencesp.split(s):
        if resentencesp.match(i) and slist:
            slist[-1] += i
        elif i:
            slist.append(i)
    return slist
```

I ignored the pattern `'^unused.*'` in my case. Would it be possible to configure that one as ignored by default? It's a pretty widely accepted convention, and anyway I would be awfully surprised if anyone complained that they were expecting to see warnings about unused variables named "unused".

A changelog sounds like a good idea to me. I'm a fan of the format suggested by https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.0.0/

The changes are that the `run()` method returns when the main task exits.  Any remaining tasks would be kept around and resumed should the`run()` method be reinvoked.  Otherwise, they'd simply be cancelled on kernel shutdown.   Note: to my knowledge, there is no "unceremonious killing" in Curio.  Tasks are always terminated via cancellation, even on kernel shutdown.  This includes daemon tasks.

That said, I'm now not so sure on getting rid of daemon tasks.  I looked into it yesterday and it caused all sorts of tests to fail.  These can be fixed by properly waiting for all child tasks to finish (e.g., adding explicit `task.join()` calls).    I guess I'd have to think about how important it is for a parent to properly wait for all of its child tasks to finish.
Pushed a possible fix for this.  Try and let me know if the problem goes away.

@kylekeppler All of the methods marked for deprecation have an @deprecated decorator around them:

```
    # Deprecated methods, will be removed in the future
    # =================================================
    @deprecated('v')
    def _get_v(self):
        return self.v
```

@saylenty did a nice job on the decorators. Basically whenever you use one of the deprecated methods you get the follow message:

```
packet._get_v()

Using non-alphanumeric characters in variable names rarely has a good outcome. It is better (explicit) to store the relationships between variables in dictionaries or other suitable data structures dedicated to that purpose. Allowing strange variable names will eventually lead to some difficulty, even if a conflict does not currently exist.

For example, suppose that a new external and compiled tool is interfaced, but it takes as input only simple variable names. Then, a translation would be needed anyway, adding an extra level of complexity. Flat is better than nested, and simple better than complex (PEP 20). Such translations are easier to deal with at the surface, meaning outside PyEDA, and where the user has easier access and knows better the context.

Hi,

I have some grand plans for this, but haven't yet implemented any of them.

First I would like to divide this into two categories. The first one, which seem to be the one you describe, is to generate code from an existing description, like generating RTL or NGC files with CoreGen from cgp and xco files. For that I want new sections in the core file. To allow for several CoreGen files, I think something like the syntax below would work

[coregen.myfancyipcore]
cgp_file=input_file1.cgp
xco_file=input_file2.xco
ngc_file=output_file1.ngc

The other category is when we are free to define the configuration files ourselves. An example of this is the Wishbone interconnect generator in wb_intercon. For those cases I envision cores being able to register themselves as "generators", which mean that they will tell FuseSoc they have the ability to generate code, either from the command-line, or on-the-fly with via a section in the .core file. To examplify this with wb_intercon_gen:

Today we call wb_intercon_gen manually with /path/to/wb_intercon_gen <description file> <output file> <module name>

By adding the following section to wb_intercon.core FuseSoC will be aware that wb_intercon is cabaple of generating code (exact format of the section is not really defined yet)

[generator]
name = wb_intercon_gen
description = Creates a Wishbone Interconnect from a description
config_file = Configuration file
output_file = Output file
module_name = Module name

and we can now generate the code with fusesoc gen wb_intercon_gen <description file> <output file> <module name>.

We can also add a section to the core that wants to use autogenerated code, to build that on-the-fly with something like this

(in toplevel .core file)
[gen.wb_intercon]
generator = wb_intercon_gen
config_file = data/wb_intercon.conf
output_file = rtl/verilog/wb_intercon.v
module_name=wb_intercon

This will hopefully allow us to reuse all code generators that people have built over the years.

Well, these are still just ideas, since I haven't gotten around actually implementing any of it, but I would be interested in knowing if that would be sufficient for your use case

Hi,
I just ran the code using the `GaussianNB` instead of `XGBoost`, and it seems to be working fine. The full code is

```python
from mlxtend.data import iris_data
from mlxtend.classifier import StackingCVClassifier
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = iris_data()

clf1 = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1)
clf2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)
clf3 = GaussianNB()

#gb = xgb.XGBClassifier( learning_rate =0.1, n_estimators=1000, max_depth=5, min_child_weight=1,
#gamma=0,subsample=0.8,colsample_bytree=0.8,objective= 'multi:softprob',
#nthread=4,scale_pos_weight=1,seed=27)

sclf = StackingCVClassifier(classifiers=[clf1, clf2],
use_probas=True,
meta_classifier=clf3,
random_state=42)

for clf, label in zip([clf1, clf2, sclf], 
                      ['KNN', 'Random Forest', 'StackingClassifier']):

    scores = cross_val_score(clf, X, y, 
                             cv=3, scoring='accuracy')
    print("Accuracy: %0.2f (+/- %0.2f) [%s]" 
          % (scores.mean(), scores.std(), label))
```



I suspect there's maybe an incompatibility with the `xgb.XGBClassifier`. What do you get if you just run the  last part, with XGBoost instead of the `StackingCVClassifier`?

```python
for clf, label in zip([clf1, clf2, sclf], 
                      ['KNN', 'Random Forest', 'StackingClassifier']):

    scores = cross_val_score(clf, X, y, 
                             cv=3, scoring='accuracy')
    print("Accuracy: %0.2f (+/- %0.2f) [%s]" 
          % (scores.mean(), scores.std(), label))
```

Also, could you check the output of `sclf.fit(X, y).predict(X).shape`? It should return `(49352,)` on your dataset.

The second connection is using the same "pool of connections" of the first one. You must specify a different pool in the connection or use the conn.strategy.terminate() method before creating the second connection:

```
from ldap3 import Server, Connection, REUSABLE, ObjectDef
server = 'edir1'
username = 'cn=admin,o=resources'
pwd = 'password'

conn = Connection(server, username, pwd, client_strategy=REUSABLE, pool_name='POOL1')
conn.bind()
obj_def = ObjectDef(['inetOrgPerson'], conn)
print(conn.strategy.pool)
print(obj_def)
conn.unbind()
# conn.strategy.terminate()  # alternative to using 2 different pools

conn = Connection(server, username, pwd, client_strategy=REUSABLE, pool_name='POOL2')
conn.bind()
obj_def = ObjectDef(['inetOrgPerson'], conn)
print(obj_def)
conn.unbind()
```

The ldap3 libray keeps the pool of threaded connections after the unbind() method because you could open the same connection again. If you really want to destroy the pool use the conn.strategy.terminate() method that closes all the threads. If you specify different pools for different connections you can use both pools simultaneously.

Hey- what's probably happening here is that the automatic generation of face normals through cross products (since OFF doesn't include normals) detected a zero- area face and removed it.

You can verify this by checking the datastore (before accessing mesh.faces):
```
print(m._data['faces'].shape)
```

The reason face normals are auto-generated and degenerate faces are removed when you access mesh.faces is so that there is no chance of getting an inconsistent shape between mesh.face_normals and mesh.faces

I agree the silent alteration is potentially questionable, however the alternatives seems to me to be bad:
 - have mesh.face_normals potentially include NaN entries (would dump warnings everywhere and be annoying).
 - don't do the check when accessing mesh.faces (which would mean mesh.faces and mesh.face_normals potentially have different shapes depending on access order)
- keep doing the same thing but output a log.warning when there's a degenerate face (this could be OK)
- do the check but raise an error when there's a degenerate face (annoying)

I'm open to suggestions :)

I'm sorry for the huge amount of changes in a single pull request, Dave!

Initially I just wanted to add brotlipy to `extras_require` so that WhiteNoise and brotlipy can be installed together and it's clearer where the dependency comes from.

Then I discovered #154 and decided to replace brotlipy with Brotli.

The implementation itself was quickly done, but I also wanted to provide a clean testing setup. So I added `extras` to `tox.ini` and the `constraints.txt` file. The main idea was to reduce the number of places where the Brotli version is defined to keep the setup DRY. Also a library should not pin it's (optional) dependencies, so I didn't do this in `setup.py` and used pip's constraint feature.

Then I discovered that the test setup for Travis and AppVeyor was not using tox but repeated many steps. So I decided to convert both to tox.

Finally I ran into problems with Python 3.3 on Travis CI. Since Python 3.3 has passed it's end of life in September 2017 and the next WhiteNoise release will already contain breaking changes I made the decision to drop Python 3.3 support.

If you don't agree with any of the changes I made please let me know and I'll update the pull request.

I used a set because testing for membership is O(1) as opposed to lists which are O(N). I don't think going back to a list is a good idea. Also think about how you're going to write to this file. If you only write when the program is quit via ``q``, you will be losing history on ``ctrl-c``.

Here's one approach that uses file appends to avoid having to overwrite the whole file after each time. There may be other approaches as well.

1. Read the file in as a list.
2. If the list length is over N, overwrite the file with only the most recent N links.
3. Convert the list to a set
4. When a new link is clicked, add it to the set AND append the link directly to the end of the file.

As for the number of links, I think 100-200 would probably be enough.

pyodbc is using PyUnicode_GET_SIZE() to calculate the column size. This returns the number of code units, so on Windows which uses UTF-16 exclusively, surrogate pairs count as 2, and thus the column size is calculated correctly at least for SQL Server which also uses UTF-16.

On Linux, where Python normally uses UTF-32, all code points are equivalent to code units, which leads it to calculate a "true" Unicode-character count of 1, even for those which would be surrogate pairs in UTF-16, but this disagrees with what SQL Server's encoding requires.

The color used for the data points in a chart series is determined by the theme of the presentation. It is not defined directly in the chart unless it is set explicitly to override the theme color.

The first series receives the *Accent 1* color, the second receives *Accent 2*, and so on. When the six accent colors are exhausted, a variant of each color (initially a lighter tint) is used in the same sequence.

So the most straightforward method of changing chart colors is to change the theme color palette of the starting presentation, (i.e. template .pptx file).

There is often a misconception about `protoc` include path, package name, .proto files structure on the file system and modules structure in a Python project. And I don't know good docs explaining all that things.

1. That package name in .proto file in Python is used only to namespace messages and services, e.g. `some.package.MessageName` or `/some.package.ServiceName/MethodName`, to avoid conflicts with names. Nothing else.

2. `protoc` assumes, that generated Python modules **must** match .proto files structure, and it generates imports between these modules accordingly. Example:
```
some
    package
        foo.proto
        bar.proto
```
And you want to import message from foo.proto in bar.proto:
```protobuf
// you inside some/package/bar.proto file
import 'some/package/foo.proto';
```
This import system somewhat similar to the import system of Python, and here is what you will get after running `protoc -I. --python_out=. some/package/*.proto`:
```
some
    package
        foo_pb2.py
        bar_pb2.py
```
As you see, files structure is the same, and imports are almost the same in the `bar_pb2.py`:
```python
from some.package import foo_pb2 as some_dot_package_dot_foo__pb2
```
3. Include path lets also find out where your package begins, so you will be able to use structure like this:
```
my-proto-files
    some
        package
            foo.proto
            bar.proto
```
And you will be able to compile them with this command:
```
$ protoc -Imy-proto-files --python_out=. my-proto-files/some/package/*.proto
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```
But this doesn't changes structure of generated files, it would be the same.

4. `--python_out` and other `--*_out` options should point to the sources root directory. It is wrong to generate files inside another package like this:
```
protoc -I my-proto-files --python_out=src/project/protos my-proto-files/some/package/*.proto
                                          ^^^^^^^
```
... where ``project`` is a root Python package of your project.

And it is wrong to assume that you will be able to get project structure like this:
```
src
    project
        __init__.py
        protos
            __init__.py
            some
                package
                    foo_pb2.py
                    ...
```
Imports will be broken. In order to have project structure like this, you should use the same structure for your .proto files. Or maybe at some point this issue will be fixed in the protoc compiler.

So, in your case, you can store .proto file as `protos/hellowold/hellowold.proto`, and generate python modules with this command:
```
$ protoc -Iprotos --python_out=. --python_grpc_out=. protos/helloworld/helloworld.proto
```

Libconf will automatically switch to using the "L" suffix for numbers outside of the 32bit integer value. So the following would force an "L" suffix:
```
libconf.dumps({'key': 100000000000000000})
```
A smaller number, such as 1000000, would not get an "L" suffix, since it comfortably fits into a normal-sized integer.

Okay, this is actually a bit amusing.  Turns out, there's already code in the plugin to do this.  

What you do is:
1. Go into Calibre's config directory (Preferences -> Misc -> Open calibre's config dir, typically something like `C:\Users\user_name\AppData\Roaming\calibre` on windows).  
1. From there, go down into `resources\images`.
1. Create a directory `FanFicFare`
1. Copy your icon image into the `FanFicFare` dir as `icon.png`
1. Restart Calibre.

I learned to make Calibre plugins by (ahem) _borrowing_ code from kiwidude's existing plugins.  I believe many if not most of the other plugins available did something similar.  Which means that the same approach will work for all of my plugins and, I suspect, most of the others as well.

Further, it looks like Calibre's theme creation process will include plugin dirs and images, so themes could even include icons for plugins directly.

Could I take advantage of your goodwill a little more and just double check I'm using this in the correct way. Like the example I gave above but I want to be able to track a response back to what initiated it. I'm currently using the `streamer` as a key. So I'm assuming that that a streamer is created per task entered into the manager and that same streamer is returned when values are yielded from that task.

for example:

```python
import random

from asyncio import sleep, run
from aiostream.manager import StreamerManager



async def go(error=False):
    yield 0
    await sleep(1)
    yield 50
    if error:
        raise RuntimeError()
    await sleep(1)
    yield 100


async def main():
    async with StreamerManager() as manager:
        parents = {}

        for task in [go(), go(), go()]:
            task_streamer = await manager.enter(task)
            parents[task_streamer] = task

        async for streamer, getter in manager.completed():
            try:
                result = getter()
            except StopAsyncIteration:
                print('complete')
                del parents[streamer]
            except RuntimeError:
                print('dead')
            else:
                if result == 50:
                    error = random.choice([True, False])
                    new_task = go(error=error)
                    task_streamer = await manager.enter(new_task)
                    parents[task_streamer] = new_task
                print(result, 'called by', parents[streamer])
                manager.schedule(streamer)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    run(main())
```

Nice, I didn't see `Msg()` in the docs. I definitely see how it could get unmanageable, but I think you could go a lot further before you've gone too far. I'm pushing for this because, in my use case at least, a schema validation failure *always* leads to someone trying to understand how to fix the problem. It seems like without too much work you could make that debugging process far easier in >50% of cases by leaning a *little* more towards too much information rather than too little.

There are two commonly missing pieces of information in the errors I've seen: the value that was provided, and the expected value.

For the first one, including the string representation of the invalid value would be plenty in most cases. `voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: invalid list value @ data['top_level_key'][0]` should become `voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: invalid list value 'foo' @ data['top_level_key'][0]`. fwiw I greatly appreciate the work that you've done to provide the path to the invalid data; that's something that most other libraries don't do. But in some cases the debugging will be quickest if you just do the obvious thing and repr the value.

As for conveying the rule that was broken, that's the place where I agree most that efforts could quickly become bulky and futile. However, even doing what you suggested above and truncating it at some number of characters you deem the maximum, eg. "`Expected one of {1: <function __main__.foo>}, {1: [2, 3, 4]}, <...truncated>`" would work quite well in a lot of cases.

@fgervais this is possible but not implemented. When creating an instance of BAC0, you are in fact defining a BACnet device with a "local device" object. This object is built with a device ID, a vendorName, etc... and could be supplied with variables at the time of creation.

If you want to play with that, have a look to `BAC0\scripts\Base.py` and `BAC0\scripts\Lite.py`.
Then look at something like : https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/blob/master/samples/MultiStateValueObject.py
It shows the creation of a MultistateValueObject : 
```
 # make a multistate value object
    msvo = MultiStateValueObject(
        objectIdentifier=('multiStateValue', 1),
        objectName='My Special Object',
        presentValue=1,
        numberOfStates=3,
        stateText=ArrayOf(CharacterString)(['red', 'green', 'blue']),
        )
    _log.debug("    - msvo: %r", msvo)

    # add it to the device
    this_application.add_object(msvo)
```
As you see, you create the object you want...then you add it to the application.

In BAC0, it is referenced to `self.this_application`

Future access to this object will have to be defined (like if you want to access it by the square bracket shortcut...) and I never been so far with BAC0.... as I always use it as a client software, without local variables.

Un peu d'huile de coude.... and you should be able to reach your goal. Joel made some improvements to bacpypes so when you create an object, default parameters will be included. Look into `bacpypes\local folder`

Hope it helps

self.assertEqual(func('{{a|b={{c}}}}'), [('a', OrderedDict((('b', '{{c}}'), ))), ('c', OrderedDict())])
vs
self.assertEqual(func('{{a|b={{c}}}}'), [('c', OrderedDict()), ('a', OrderedDict((('b', '{{c}}'), )))])

When a model is saved by calling it's save() method, it is added to the
current revision. If the model has been registered with any `follow`
arguments, then those relations will also be followed and added to the
revision.

http://django-reversion.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#registration-api

To use multi-table inheritance with django-reversion, you have to register
both the parent and the child model, and for both of them specify the link
to the other model in `follow`.

For example:

class Parent(models.Model):
    pass

class Child(Parent):
    pass

reversion.register(Child, follow=("parent_ptr",))
reversion.register(Parent, follow=("child",))

Have you set up your model correctly?

In your request `/api/v4/users//tokens` You seem to be missing the `user_id`
`/api/v4/users/{user_id}/tokens`
(Not 100% sure, but if you want to create a token for your user, probably `/api/v4/users/me/tokens` works)

The response you get, indicates that you are missing your token (you get one from the server when you login) in your request. this library takes care of that everytime a request is run.
If you would want to do that manually you need to do include it in the header:
``` python
# Assuming you set up the mattermostdriver somewhere
foo = Driver({})
foo.login() # After this, the token is saved in `Driver.client.token`
requests(
'https://<my_mattermost_URL>/api/v4/users//tokens',
    # This is needed so mattermost knows you are allowed to do that 
    header={"Authorization": "Bearer {token:s}".format(token=foo.client.token)
    # Not sure about the cookie stuff you are doing
    cookies=foo.client.cookise,
    json=payload
)
```

But with the version I just released (4.3.1) you can just do
`foo.users.create_user_access_token('user_id', options=payload)`
and should be fine too.

@nursix you're right. Let me check with the other developers.

>> We should think about required fields (at least those that aren't also computed fields) and computed fields separately.
> 
> Would make sense if the issue wasn't related to the use of the first in combination with the other ones.

I'm not sure what you mean. The reported issue involves `required` fields but no computed fields. We should be able to calculate computed fields before the `_before_` callbacks but still check for `required` fields after. What is wrong with that approach? I guess it's a little less efficient, as you have to loop through the fields one more time (though you could mitigate that by recording the required fields during the first loop and looping over only those fields in the second loop).

> And, for the records, just because something worked in the past doesn't mean it worked correctly.

Right. But just because we now might prefer to support one set of use cases over another set of use cases does not mean we should therefore label the old behavior a bug and use that as an excuse to break backward compatibility.

> I can't see how the upper workflow is right, considering that:
> 
> if you need to set a default value, you shouldn't use a callback for that

First, consider a slightly different callback, where the default value is only set conditionally:

    def before_insert_test(f):
        if some_condition:
            f['field1'] = 'field1'

The above could not be achieved via the `default` argument, which will always assign a value (even if it is `None`).

Second, the point is that in the past, we allowed setting a default via a callback. Just because we now don't like that option doesn't make it a bug that merits breaking backward compatibility. Furthermore, that doesn't make the workflow "wrong," it just means there is some other way to achieve the desired outcome in some cases.

> if you want to override or set a value in callbacks then there's no valid reason to set that field as required, 'cause required means the input should be given on the insert instruction to me, not injected in the flow

I don't see why `required` must take on that particular meaning. It could just as well mean a value is required before making the final submission to the database.

> And talking about meanings, I think we also have a philosophical issue here. Because to me a before_insert method should be the last thing execute before an insert, without everything happening after. In other words, I consider a before callback a sort of barrier before the operation.

First, I don't think it's a philosophical difference -- it's just that we already have some automatic behavior happening before insert, and there are therefore multiple places where callbacks could run in that process. There is no philosophical argument that says the callbacks must come before or after calculating default values or computed values -- we simply have to decide which use cases are most important to support (or we can support both by adding an additional hook, as you suggested).

Second, whatever your preference regarding the ordering of callbacks vs. defaults/computed values, it's not clear why checking required fields must come before the callbacks (again, other than to avoid the extra loop).

> Considering all of this, I think adding another callback is not a complexity issue, rather an additional 'signal' to bind to, and I still consider a better solution for the issue.

Sure, adding complexity is justified if there are real use cases worth supporting. If that's the case here, we should add another callback.

> Changing the order of operation again, will fix certain workflows, but will brake other ones.

I don't object to the new workflow, but I think we should acknowledge that the old behavior was not a bug that would justify breaking backward compatibility. Rather, we just decided we prefer the new workflow and opted to break backward compatibility to support it.

Now, if we're OK changing the web2py policy regarding backward compatibility, we should (a) be explicit about the new policy, and (b) employ a proper process to warn users and document the breakages (and recommended code changes/workarounds). We should not simply make such changes silently and allow code to break and data to be corrupted without warning.

Finally, note that in some apps, this change might not only lead to exceptions being thrown (as reported above), but also to silent corruption of data. For example, consider a `_before_insert` callback that first mutated some submitted values *before* those values were passed on to a computed field. With this change, those computed fields will now be taking in incorrect values, possibly silently corrupting the data (rather than throwing an obvious exception).

@AlexTS1980 Perhaps you can check with how I solved with the similiar issue for pytorch.
Basically it's linkable issue for /home/ICTDOMAIN/453615/anaconda2/lib/libopencv_imgcodecs.so.3.3.1.
1. detect /home/ICTDOMAIN/453615/anaconda2/lib/libopencv_imgcodecs.so.3.3.1 link symbol:
```bash
ldd /home/ICTDOMAIN/453615/anaconda2/lib/libopencv_imgcodecs.so.3.3.1
```
2. detect where system having libstdc++.so.6/libstdc++.so
```bash
sudo find / -name libstdc++.so.6
```
3. you can find actually you have 2 libstdc++.so.6 in system
*  system library - /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
*  anaconda library - /mnt/xvdb/jupyter/miniconda3/lib/./libstdc++.so.6 -> libstdc++.so.6.0.24
4. The error is for anacoda library mismatched with your system library.
* if you are on mac, using "install_name_tool -change", refer to  https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33991581/install-name-tool-to-update-a-executable-to-search-for-dylib-in-mac-os-x
* if you are on the linux/ubuntu, you can recreate symbol link for anaconda library libstdc++.so.6:
sudo ln -s -f  /mnt/xvdb/jupyter/miniconda3/lib/./libstdc++.so.6 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6.0.25

Tips: please do check and change symbol links instead of library itself. Hope it will help

@viperfx Could you give any more information as to how your code is structured? It's hard to tell just from these examples.

One thought that crossed my mind was it might be how your ZipkinAttrs are interacting with the sampling rate. You're specifying `is_sampled=False` in your ZipkinAttrs and then sampling at a very low rate (0.1% - `sample_rate` is an int between 0.0 and 100.0, where the latter is 100% sampling). If you're doing this at every level of your program, that means you're sampling independently at each of them. I.e. 0.1% of "stage3:worker" requests will have spans generated for them, _regardless of whether the corresponding "stage2:worker" request was sampled_. Ideally, you'd instead sample at the uppermost level, then propagate whether it was sampled to the lower levels.

The job list is empty, and the `CompoundListFiled` class will throw an error. It throws an error because it tries to create a list resource by using list comprehension and the there are no items. I will fix this by returning empty list if there no present jobs in task. This will fix the issue of NoneType error and the necessity to catch errors as they happen.

Annoy relies on random memory accesses. I bet, it's several dozens if not hundreds of CPU cycles on top of the actual computation. From my experience with optimizing memory layouts, for 100d data this typically means that L2s are twice as slow compared to the case when data is accessed sequentially. So, making L2s 10% faster won't make a big difference.

However, if you make your L2s (or cosine's) 4times as slower, you will probably see the difference. This would happen if the compiler fails to vectorize the loops. Modern GCC compilers are good at vectorizing L2s and the cosine. However, some older versions and Clang aren't.

I would suggest not including the key as part of the presigned url like so:
```
url = s3client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={
        "Bucket": aws_data["bucket"],
        "Key": upload_filename,
        "SSECustomerAlgorithm": "AES256"})
```

Then when you use the presigned url you need to include the appropriate headers to the request:
```
curl --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-algorithm:AES256' --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key:<key>' --header 'x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key-md5:<keymd5>'<presignedurl>
```

When you make the request through a boto3 client like s3.get_object it automatically adds these headers for you. You will need to provide the headers when you use the presigned url though requests, curl, etc.

@inspirehep/inspire-content @inspirehep/inspire-dir 

Given the following facts:
* In the last 6 months only ~0.01% users queried INSPIRE using arXiv categories (or any category at all)
* arXiv categories are not available for all types of documents

Given the following thoughts:
* Faceting for ~10 categories (INSPIRE) is nicer than faceting for ~30 (arXiv)
* Using neural network (magpie) to guess ~10 categories (INSPIRE) is more precise and reliable than ~30 categories (arXiv)

It is proposed here to simplify the data model and hence curation inteface in the following way:
* [ ] store only INSPIRE categories
* [X] upon ingestion external categories (e.g. arXiv) are mapped to INSPIRE ones
* [X] if no external category we use magpie to guess one based on abstract
* [ ] upon migration from legacy we preserve INSPIRE categories and we map existing arXiv ones into INSPIRE.
* [ ] Present to users a facet based on INSPIRE categories.

Some random thoughts on this one as a maintainer:

1. I don’t have an issue if the change is made to include dependencies of a VCS dependency. I would probably prefer that, in fact.
2. Making the change now would throw everyone relying on this off. Not a good idea unless we can provide an alternative.
3. This is like the venv in project vs centraised directory debate. I wish it were this other way around, and would most definitely fight for it if I were around when it was decided. But I was not, and the ship sailed. Too late to change that now.
4. Maybe, in the future, we can run a survey, put up a deprecation notice, and make the switch. I look forward to the day.
5. Alas, we are so far from that day. There are so many things broken, and so few people with so little time to fix them. 
6. This has a workaround, and is a design decision. We will visit it later.

It’s resource management at the end. I don’t think anyone would reasonably say this suggestion is an objectively wrong, but on the other hand, you can’t say the other behaviour is, either (you can claim one is _more_ correct than the other, but that is a different thing). With limited resource, however, we can only ask you to drop this. We can’t, and don’t want to deal with the consequences of the change.

I would like to mention that the pull request functionality is always open, and anyone is also free to comment on most issues to provide help and insights. Join us to help bring Pipenv to maturity, and then we will be able to revisit, and maybe correct mistakes we made along the way. Thank you.

Hiding the problem from the user is not helpful.

I think there's a more insidious problem here - with pip-installed packages (or any other installer that omits `__init__.py` modules for namespace packages), a project that shares a namespace with its dependencies will run into conflicts under Python 3.3+ because the package under development will contain a `__init__.py` file in the namespace package, but the dependencies will not. For example, consider something like `zope.component`, which depends on `zope.interface`. Since the introduction of #805, if one were to install zope.component using `setup.py develop`, `zope` would be loaded by the NamespaceLoader for `zope.interface` (assuming it was pip installed), but by SourceFileLoader for `zope.component` (because there's no opportunity for `zope/__init__.py` to be omitted). For the project under development to be installed in a way that's consistent with its dependencies, the `zope/__init__.py` would have to be removed from the filesystem. Perhaps the project could be reconfigured not to include that file at all, but that would eliminate the ability to use `find_packages` (at least until #97 can be solved). I'm also unsure if this causes conflicts with dependencies installed as eggs.

Some of the motivations that led me to filing this ticket originally:

1. It's better in principle for packages to declare their dependencies rather than vendor them.
2. pip already vendors its dependencies, and pip is the blessed package that has special handling for bootstrapping (through ensurepip, etc). Setuptools should be able to rely on that.
3. Updating vendored packages without making mistakes is much more difficult.
4. Vendoring made it impossibly complex to extract pkg_resources as a separate package (because both would need vendoring, and that causes more nesting).
5. Pull requests for code in vendored packages would have to be handled and rejected.
6. A fallback technique needed to be developed and maintained to allow system package managers to unvendor the dependencies.
7. The vendoring technique itself isn't a well-defined process, so itself developed over several iterations and was inconsistent with the vendoring technique pip uses.
8. Vendoring increases the risk and challenges involved with adopting new dependencies.
9. Vendoring masks the actual dependencies (nothing was declared in install_requires and equivalent metadata).
10. The PyPA seeks to make setuptools a tool for building, pkg_resources for metadata access, and for these to be treated mostly like normal packages.

There are probably others, but those are the ones that come to mind. The main downside that comes to mind, aside from the various edge cases that are being addressed, is that of bootstrapping from source, especially when the dependencies require setuptools to build from source, being discussed in #980.

Finally tracked it down.

I suspected that pip may be the issue because, that was the only other pkg_resources module I could find in my file system. (also there is no other setupttools version installed anywhere)

However, in my case I have a namespace module `org` with an` __init__.py` file containing:
`__import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)`

When my app starts up, something is importing stdlib `copy` module which tries to import `from org.python.core import PyStringMap` which will load my `__init__.py` from my namespace package, which again tries to use `copy` (within pkg_resources/_vendor/pyparsing) and then somewhere further up in the call stack, something was catching the AttributeError on import and seemed to have left some of the module imports in a funny state. etc....

Essentially, using `__import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)` created an recursive import chain in my case.

Lesson to take form this. Don't use `org` as namespace package or use `pkgutil.extend_path` instead of `pkg_resources.declare_namespace` if unavoidable.

(Still not clear why using `__import__` works but `from . import` doesn't though.)

Pykafka's [partition assignment logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/ceb6c9c1700b7c927e2077c66f9c31205bb2890f/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L431-L452) was last updated prior to the standardization of different assignment strategies, around version `0.8.2`. It follows the "rebalancing algorithm" laid out [here](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#impl_consumerrebalance), which until 0.10 was the only standard strategy available. From my read of the `roundrobin` and `range` strategy documentation, it appears that this algorithm is the `range` strategy. If that's indeed the case, we can simply change `pykafkaassignmentstrategy` to `range` and this problem will probably disappear. If the algorithms are subtly different, we may end up with some partition contention between pykafka and the golang consumers. Support for `roundrobin` can be added in the future, but is not strictly a blocker for using pykafka alongside other consumer implementations.

Please note this patch reverses the logic, Since connecting to hosts is a more expensive operation than calculating things locally, this code minimises this.

The old code connected for each keyring, to each mon until the mon returned with the keyring.

The new logic code connects to a mon and then retrieves all keyrings, from that one mon, and only if it fails to retrieve all keyrings does it then to the next mon.

This should make the number of connections created and destroyed reduce from 5 to 1. As well as the benefit listed above.

### 4.0 vs 3.1+4.0 support
I think it might be possible to keep supporting both 3.1 and 4.0 with the same code base without too much hassle. The main issue in supporting both is properly the settings that have been renamed and the upper vs lowercase names.

### Lowercase or uppercase settings names (independent of v4.0 vs 3.1+4.0)
It seems from the release notes that it's a valid use case to keep using uppercase names with a prefix (see "For Django users and others who want to keep uppercase names"). As long as we load the celery configuration from the Flask app I think it makes sense to keep using uppercase variable names with ``CELERY_`` prefix, since this is the pattern used by FLASK. Alternatively if we go with 4.0 only, then we could consider loading the celery configuration from a separate file.

### 4.0 only support
If we go with 4.0 only support, I would first make a new release 0.2.3 which requires Celery v3.1.25 - to ensure compatibility between 3.1 and 4.0 messages, and then afterwards make a 1.0 release for Celery 4.

Add this to the right module for me I put it under utils/views.py
```
from myapp.users.serializers import CustomUserSerializer

from rest_framework import generics, serializers, permissions, status
from rest_framework.response import Response

from djoser import serializers, settings, utils, signals

from rest_framework_jwt.settings import api_settings
from datetime import datetime
from calendar import timegm

class CustomRegistrationView(generics.CreateAPIView):
    """
    Use this endpoint to register new user.
    """
    serializer_class = serializers.serializers_manager.get('user_registration')
    permission_classes = (
        permissions.AllowAny,
    )

    def perform_create(self, serializer):
        user = serializer.save()
        signals.user_registered.send(sender=self.__class__, user=user, request=self.request)
        if settings.get('SEND_ACTIVATION_EMAIL'):
            self.send_activation_email(user)
        elif settings.get('SEND_CONFIRMATION_EMAIL'):
            self.send_confirmation_email(user)
        return user

    def create(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        serializer = self.get_serializer(data=request.data)
        serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
        user = self.perform_create(serializer)
        headers = self.get_success_headers(serializer.data)
        token = create_token(user)
        content = {
            'token': token,
            'user': CustomUserSerializer(user).data
        }
        return Response(content, status=status.HTTP_201_CREATED, headers=headers)

    def send_activation_email(self, user):
        email_factory = utils.UserActivationEmailFactory.from_request(self.request, user=user)
        email = email_factory.create()
        email.send()

    def send_confirmation_email(self, user):
        email_factory = utils.UserConfirmationEmailFactory.from_request(self.request, user=user)
        email = email_factory.create()
        email.send()

    def get_email_context(self, user):
        context = super(RegistrationView, self).get_email_context(user)
        context['url'] = settings.get('ACTIVATION_URL').format(**context)
        return context

#create_token is based on lines from: https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/477d5887abbbce7a9d11dc827d954970d7c66bd7/rest_framework_jwt/serializers.py#L55-L66
def create_token(user):
    jwt_payload_handler = api_settings.JWT_PAYLOAD_HANDLER
    jwt_encode_handler = api_settings.JWT_ENCODE_HANDLER

    payload = jwt_payload_handler(user)
    # Include original issued at time for a brand new token,
    # to allow token refresh
    if api_settings.JWT_ALLOW_REFRESH:
        payload['orig_iat'] = timegm(
            datetime.utcnow().utctimetuple()
        )

    return jwt_encode_handler(payload)
```

A few comments on your note, without reading the code:

> The input images are assumed to be square, as these produce square Fourier transform “pixels”
> which is required by InterpolatedKImage. I think this can fairly easily be made more general though.

Right.  I don't think this is a horrible limitation for a first implementation though.

> The input images are assumed to all have the same wcs; i.e., there’s no attempt to handle dithering 
> or rotation. This is probably okay for now, since multi-band HST data is typically drizzled onto a 
> common pixel grid, though we may want to extend this in the future.

Ditto.  This seems like something to document but perhaps leave as-is for the initial implementation.  (As we've seen before, basically everything we do is found to require some re-designing once people are using it, so I don't feel it's too bad to have some minor limitations in the code given that we basically expect to have to fiddle with it more.)

> The code is written to take ChromaticObjects as input PSFs for each image. I have recently realized, 
> however, that it still runs fine if one just asserts GSObjects as the input PSFs, i.e., if one ignores the 
> fact that blue and red stars in the same filter have different effective PSFs in the HST images. It’s 
> possible that capturing the inter-band chromatic PSF differences while ignoring the intra-band 
> chromatic PSF differences is sufficient (and it’s definitely much simpler), albeit somewhat less 
> rigorous.

Sorry if I am missing something, but it seems to me that this would be easy to test using some realistic stellar SEDs?  (i.e., test the difference between proper treatment of chromatic PSFs vs. achromatic GSObject PSFs)  I feel that this is more important to nail down in an initial implementation, since in practice for HST we generally have an achromatic PSF estimate.

> Currently, the noise propagation stuff is computed for every ChromaticRealGalaxy. If there are use 
> cases where one doesn’t care about whitening the noise, then this is an unnecessary time sink.

Could have a kwarg to shut this off?  (but default is on)

> I couldn’t think of a good way to write something analogous to CorrelatedNoise.convolveWith(), or at 
> least something that automatically gets called when a ChromaticRealGalaxy is convolved with 
> another profile. This is because turning the CovarianceSpectrum into a CorrelatedNoise object 
> requires that the output filter be specified, which isn’t done until drawImage is called. So instead, 
> there’s a toNoise() method of CovarianceSpectrum with arguments for both a PSF and a Bandpass. 
> I.e., you have to explicitly convolve the noise in parallel with the ChromaticRealGalaxy; it doesn’t 
> happen automatically like for RealGalaxy.

Hmmmm.  It would be nice to have that as an option.  What if the Chromatic convolver had a kwarg for the output filter (optional, but if you know it, it could let ChromaticRealGalaxy handle this behind the scenes)?

Finally - I am not loving the ```CovarianceSpectrum``` name.  I don't know why but I'm not finding it obvious/intuitive.  What about ```ChromaticCorrelatedNoise```? (just like we have ```OpticalPSF``` and ```ChromaticOpticalPSF```, ```RealGalaxy``` and ```ChromaticRealGalaxy```, etc.)

>Likewise the bandpasses for the different filters that will be used (I think these are already the ones Josh loaded into examples/data, so this might already be ready to go.

I believe the throughputs I added a while ago are specifically for airmass=1.2.  I think it's worth putting in a basic atmospheric extinction function (even if we neglect the specifics of water vapor, aerosols, etc.) to get this right as a function of airmass.

Relatedly, I think it makes sense to specify the atmospheric PSF FWHM specifically for observations at zenith and at 500 nm, and then scale to whatever elevation and wavelength (if using the chromatic code) or filter effective wavelength (if rendering achromatically; this should probably be an option for speed) is requested.

I managed to fix this by modifying src/guake/guake_app.py and replacing:
```
        if platform.linux_distribution()[0].lower() == "ubuntu":
```
with:
```
        if os.environ.get('DESKTOP_SESSION') == "ubuntu":
```

Apparently the DESKTOP_SESSION is "ubuntu" when the user is using Unity, but it's different for the other Ubuntu flavors (it's "gnome" for Ubuntu GNOME, "mate" for Ubuntu MATE, "Lubuntu" for Lubuntu, etc.). I'm not sure if this is the best approach, but it works for me :)

The code all look good. One thing I want to point out is that this aiohttp support doesn't work with tracing subtasks. e.g: You cannot trace any extra async functions in your request handler. Here is the reason: Say the server creates task A for an incoming request from user A, then in your request handler there is another async function, task A will create task B for this function execution. The SDK cannot record this async function because when the SDK creates a subsegment for this function, the SDK doesn't know this subsegment's parent since the context for task B is empty. 

In order to get nested async working you need to monkey patch `asyncio.create_task` so that it propagates context from current task to the newly created task. 

I just want to make sure you are aware of this issue and it could still cause you problems if later on you add some async functions in your request handler and try to trace these functions. If your PR is not intended to support this advanced use case then some reasoning and limitation in the documentation should be sufficient.

@yoavg OK, I tried to make two changes to this and am pretty much satisfied.

1. Tests are added for the basic case, and seem to be working.

2. I've disabled the combination of checkpointing + dynamic memory allocation. The reason for this is because the current implementation will result in the number of pools increasing every time checkpointing rolls back to a previous InternalMemoryPool, and the only good way I could think about fixing this is freeing the memory pools every time we roll back, which seems quite costly in the standard use-case for checkpointing (where we calculate, then throw out results repeatedly during search). I think that it's OK to have this off because a) checkpointing is an advanced enough option that people using it can probably also specify `--dynet-mem` correctly, and b) I have some plans about how to do memory optimization in a better way than checkpointing, which hopefully will be coming in the near future.

As for

> How can I programmatically verify that the background threads are working correctly

Instead of calling

```python
self.subscriber.subscribe(
    self.subscription_path, 
    callback=self.callback,
    flow_control=self.flow_control)
```

you could call

```python
policy = self.subscriber.subscribe(
    self.subscription_path, 
    flow_control=self.flow_control)
future = policy.open(self.callback)
```

and then you can check if the subscriber is still working by checking `future.done()`. If `future.done()` is `False` (i.e. if it failed), then `future.exception()` will tell you what the failure is. The only way `future.done()` can be true in the case of "success" is if you call `policy.close()`. (This was not true before `0.29.3/0.29.4` due to a bug.)

You could also do:

```python
policy = self.subscriber.subscribe(
    self.subscription_path, 
    callback=self.callback,
    flow_control=self.flow_control)
future = policy.future
```

though `policy.future` (which is a read-only `@property` for the `policy._future` attribute/member) is subject to change. (If you call `close()` it will be set to `None`.)

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/releases/tag/pubsub-0.29.1
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/releases/tag/pubsub-0.29.2
[3]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/releases/tag/pubsub-0.29.3
[4]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/releases/tag/pubsub-0.29.4
[5]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/releases/tag/pubsub-0.30.0
[6]: https://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pubsub/releases.html

    text = xml.decode('utf-8')
    text = text.replace("</w:hyperlink>","")
    text = re.sub('<w:hyperlink[^>]*>', "", text)
    return text.encode('utf-8')

@oddjobz<https://github.com/oddjobz> yes, it's possible to pin versions (and/or use <= some version). However, pip doesn't have a "real" dependency-resolver :(

The usual advice I've seen is to pin versions for "application-like" things, and leave them open-ended for "library-like" things to make installs more-likely to play nicely (e.g. if Autobahn pinned to Twisted==16.0.0 but some other library pinned to Twisted==something_else). pip just installs the first one it sees...(i.e. if the first dependency it finds that mentions Twisted asks for 14.0.0, that's what it'll install even if a later one asks for 16.1.0).

So, it may indeed make sense to pin exact, tested versions for Crossbar -- but I don't think it makes sense to pin versions for the Autobahn Python library itself.

So, the issue that came up in #82 was actually a matter of `autopep8` enforcing a recommendation of [PEP 257](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/).
It apparently states that a class docstring should be followed by a blank line ([citation](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/#multi-line-docstrings)).

`flake8` doesn't seem to support PEP257 enforcement.

If we talk about bringing in a second linting tool to pacify `autopep8`, it might as well be `autopep8` in `--diff` mode anyway.

Indeed, there are many gotchas when dealing with `POSIX` emulation and `GCC` on Windows. In general people take bits from here, there and everywhere ending up with a hodge-podge solution that works only in some cases.

This is one of the reasons that `MSYS2` was started. It aims to be a cohesive `POSIX` emulation layer (light fork of `Cygwin`) which does all that it can facilitate interoperation between that layer and native Windows. As such, it has a large suite of packages (end-user software, developer tools and libraries) for both `POSIX` and native Windows `GCC` (`mingw-w64`).

You can install and use many `MSYS2` packages from `conda` too - these were added mainly to allow building `conda` packages that require a `POSIX` build system or `GCC` to be built in a repeatable way. The POSIX packages are prefixed with `m2-` and the native `GCC` (`mingw-w64`) ones are prefixed with `m2w64-`.

It's not perfect, but `POSIX` on Windows probably never will be.

A bad `ruamel_yaml` package was released to `defaults`, but was quickly rectified.

If you run into this issue, rename any recognized condarc file (most common file is a file named `.condarc` in your home directory), run `conda update ruamel_yaml`, and then you can put your `.condarc` file back.  The crucial part is that conda not see any configuration files to read and parse.

Since y'all liked the quartiles of measured run times so much, here as some new ones:

TLDR; `conda activate` will take only 59 % of the previous run time when everything is updated to their (pre-) release versions.

#!/usr/bin/env python

from __future__ import print_function

from collections import OrderedDict
from itertools import chain
from os import system
from timeit import repeat


repeats = 100
row_sep = '-'
col_sep = '|'
percentiles = (0, .25, .5, .75, 1)


def import_shell_command(module):
    return "python -c 'import {}'".format(module)


runs = OrderedDict((
    ('base', ['true', "python -c 'pass'"]),
    ('base conda imports', list(map(import_shell_command, (
        'conda',
        'conda.base',
        'conda.common',
    )))),
    ('external imports', list(map(import_shell_command, (
        'inspect',
        'urllib',
        'urllib.request',
        'conda._vendor',
        'conda._vendor.toolz',
        'conda._vendor.toolz.functoolz',
        'ruamel.yaml',
    )))),
    ('conda modules with costly imports', list(map(import_shell_command, (
        'conda.common.path',
        'conda.common.url',
        'conda.common.serialize',
        'conda.common.configuration',
        'conda.base.context',
    )))),
    ('conda commands', [
        'python -m conda info',
        'python -m conda --version',
        'python -m conda shell.posix activate',
    ]),
))

def to_percentile_string(values, percentiles=percentiles, fmt='{:9.5f}'):
    values = sorted(values)
    # NOTE: Don't really care if we get a one-off-error in this context...
    percentile_values = (values[int(p * (len(values)-1))] for p in percentiles)
    return ' '.join(fmt.format(v) for v in percentile_values)


def print_sep_row(
    row_sep=row_sep, col_sep=col_sep,
    _row_len=max(max(map(len, cmds)) for cmds in runs.values())
):
    print(
        row_sep * len(to_percentile_string((0,))),
        col_sep,
        row_sep * _row_len,
        sep=row_sep,
    )


def run(category, shell_commands, print_category=True, print_header=False):
    if print_header:
        print(
            '{0:^{1}}'.format(
                f'  runtime in seconds (percentiles of {repeats} runs)',
                len(to_percentile_string((0,))),
            ),
            col_sep, 'shell command'
        )
        print(to_percentile_string(range(101), fmt='{:>9}'), col_sep)
        print_sep_row()
    if print_category:
        print('##', category)
        print_sep_row()

    for shell_command in shell_commands:
        silent_shell_command = f'{shell_command} >/dev/null 2>&1'
        times = repeat(
            lambda: system(silent_shell_command), number=1, repeat=repeats,
        )
        print(to_percentile_string(times), col_sep, shell_command)
    if print_category:
        print_sep_row()


for i, (category, shell_commands) in enumerate(runs.items()):
    run(category, shell_commands, print_header=(i == 0))

If we wanted to change this to use the six library, we would (from what I saw when experimenting with it) just check to see if it was of type `six.string_types` and then blindly cast it to `str`, which doesn't really show why the cast is necessary.

- You should not use `done()` anyway since it's a non-blocking call. Use `wait` (returns `None`) or `result` (if you expect your LRO to return something.)
- The timeout in `wait` / `result` is the Python one: we will exit with an exception if your total length on the operation is reached. By default it's `None`, i.e. wait until it's done forever.
- The `long_running_operation_timeout ` timeout is for time *between* the calls to Azure. You want to change that if you're afraid to be blacklisted by Azure by polling too much, or you want faster reaction (minimal time for LRO is this timeout: initial call + wait this timeout + first polling). Note that if `retry-after` is set by the service, this value is used (and this timeout ignored).

Occasionally when downloading part of a file we only get part of the file.
We send a request with a range header and we receive only some of the data.
When this happens ddsclient hangs waiting for the rest of the file.
The main thread is waiting for more data from the workers and the workers think they have downloaded everything. So far I have only seen this crossing network boundary similar to the issues that spawned the upload retries.

DNS is just UDP on port 53 and as tproxy proxies all UDP it will just work without the need for `--dns`. In fact, recent versions of sshuttle will exit with an error if you try to use `--dns` with `--method=tproxy`. You are probably using some older version...

```
 ./run -r some_server 10.0.0.0/8 --method=tproxy --dns --disable-ipv6
fatal: Feature dns not supported with method tproxy.
```

Maybe we should just ignore `--dns` when the method being used supports UDP, as there is no need for any specific DNS proxying.

I think It is failing from VS because the runtimeIdentifier to use during publish is not specified. You are specifying it for the commandline scenario when you pass this flag  (-r win-x64) but this is not specified in the VS publish scenario.

Solution:
1.You can either change the RuntimeIdentifiers to RuntimeIdentifier in the csproj and publish from VS should succeed.
2. You can keep the RuntimeIdentifiers in csproj but for the profile that you are publishing, you can specify the RuntimeIdentifier to use. This can be done from the profile settings UI or adding this property(RuntimeIdentifier) to the pubxml under Properties\PublishProfiles\<profilename>.pubxml

I don't think `string` issue will be that big, as in this case it would be a "hack" to go from string to a list of chars, by simply specifying `fields.List(fields.String())` on the string value in the serialized class. And if people want to serialize string, they shall really use `fields.String()`.

I believe this pull request is the first step, and then `fields.List` can be generalized into supporting all iterables. On the other hand, it is not that easy to check if something is `iterable` in python ([link](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1952464/in-python-how-do-i-determine-if-an-object-is-iterable)).

It seems that something like this can be done:

```python
    if utils.is_indexable_but_not_string(value) and not isinstance(value, dict):
        return [self.container.serialize(idx, value) for idx in range(len(value))]
    if utils.is_iterable_but_not_string(value) and not isinstance(value, dict):
        return [self.container.serialize(0, [entry]) for entry in value]
    ....
````

and `utils.is_iterable_but_not_string(value)` can be implemented something like this:
```python
    def is_iterable_but_not_string(obj):
        return hasattr(obj, "__iter__") and not not hasattr(obj, "strip")

Although there are use cases for that behavior (e.g. you are validating posted form: in that case everything is strings), I think it should be customizable. All numeric fields have "as_string" option which if enabled serializes numbers to string representation. But on deserialization it doesn't check that option and just relies on standard conversion functions to handle incorrect input. Numeric fields could be extended to account for "as_string" option and check that input value is of proper type.

Thanks @amw for bringing this PR forward. I have also struggled with bulk operation support with json:api for a while and have been following the different discussions.

What you are suggesting is to implement the experimental bulk extension as described [here](https://github.com/json-api/json-api/blob/v1.0/extensions/bulk/index.md)

This seems to have been [deprecated](http://jsonapi.org/extensions/) and json:api is properly moving to a completely different approach in version 1.1 as discussed in https://github.com/json-api/json-api/pull/1197

I think though it is still legit to implement bulk extension in DJA as DJA currently targets json:api version "1.0". I think though as it is an experimental feature and media type is different ('application/vnd.api+json; ext=bulk') bulk support logic should be separated into its own parser. Reason being as the standard doesn't allow list primary data when [creating](http://jsonapi.org/format/#crud-creating) or [updating](http://jsonapi.org/format/#crud-updating) resources so the jsonapi parser shouldn't allow it either.

Instead a bulk parser could be designed like this:

```python
import io
import json

from django.conf import settings
from rest_framework_json_api import parsers

class JSONAPIBulkParser(parsers.JSONParser):
    media_type = 'application/vnd.api+json; ext=bulk'

    def parse(self, stream, media_type=None, parser_context=None):
        data = []
        encoding = parser_context.get('encoding', settings.DEFAULT_CHARSET)
        data_collection = json.loads(stream.read().decode(encoding))

        for single_data in data_collection['data']:
            single_data = {'data': single_data}
            sub_data = super(JSONAPIBulkParser, self).parse(
                io.BytesIO(bytes(json.dumps(single_data), encoding)),
                media_type=media_type,
                parser_context=parser_context
            )
            data.append(sub_data)

        return data
```

For this parser to work a view mixin would be needed which could look something like this:

```python
class BulkExtensionMixin(object):
     def get_serializer(self, *args, **kwargs):
        is_bulk = isinstance(self.request.data, list)
        kwargs['many'] = kwargs.pop('many', is_bulk)
        return super().get_serializer(*args, **kwargs)
```

This way if a user of DJA wants to use the experimental bulk feature it would need to add new parser to `DEFAULT_PARSER_CLASSES` and extend views with mixin which actually need bulk support. In the respective class it could be clearly documented that this is an experimental feature.

What do you think?

The base unit for all the metrics displayed on the summary tab is a tile. In order to calculate the variation in error rate to match SAV, you'd need to aggregate all the cycles for each tile into a tile-level error rate, and then calculate the mean/variance on those values. This way, the variance shows the difference between tiles, as opposed to being dominated by cycle-to-cycle variability.

Please see the documentation here: http://illumina.github.io/interop/error_metrics_requirement.html for more information.

@levnach @agurfinkel is correct. If you really want to add ``src`` as an include path you could do

```cmake
list(APPEND Z3_COMPONENT_EXTRA_INCLUDE_DIRS "${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/src")
```

What you are asking to do make me slightly worried though. What are you trying to achieve? Currently the CMake build system mimics the Python build system and declares dependencies between components. This has two purposes

* Declaring that component ``A`` has a  dependency on say a component ``B`` means that the ``B`` component (and all it's dependencies) will be built before ``A``. This is necessary because some of the components generate their header files at build time and so those header files need to exist before other components try to use them.

* Adding include directories. If a component ``A`` declares a dependency on component ``B`` then ``A`` will have the source and build directory of component ``B`` (and the source and build directory of its dependencies) added to the include paths for component ``A``. I'm not a huge fan of this. I think it would be much simpler if all ``#include``s were written relative to ``src/`` i.e. ``#include "ast/fpa/fpa2bv_converter.h"`` rather than ``#include "fpa2bv_converter.h"``. Then we would only need to specify two include paths (i.e. ``${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/src`` and ``${CMAKE_BINARY_DIR}/src``). However Z3's includes aren't written this way currently and I haven't found the time to go in and fix this.

Anyway the point is the fact you are trying to add include directories makes me think you might be trying to subvert the dependencies between components. So if that's what you're doing don't do it because it will break the build in unpredictable ways.

VEX will refuse to translate a jump or branch instruction on MIPS if it does not have enough data to put an instruction in the delay slot of the jump. VEX is meant to be used as a dynamic translation engine for execution purposes, so it's hope is that you'll resume execution at the jump instruction and provide additional bytes afterwards. If you give it only a single jump instruction, it will give you an Ijk_NoDecode.

You can think of the FontVersion object as an in memory representation of the version string in the binary on instantiation.  Everything stays in memory (I don't surprise with any OT table writes, this must be called explicitly) until calling code writes whatever modifications are made to the version string directly through the object properties (e.g. you can modify each of the substring items in the FontVersion.version_string_parts list) or through the public methods.

The `unicode` type in Python 2 is the equivalent of the `str` type in Python 3. Hence the `py23` module in fonttools export a `unicode` alias that points to `unicode` in Python 2 and to `str` in Python 3. You can import that `unicode` type from `py23` module and use it, e.g., with `isinstance`.

If you want to convert a `bytes` string to a `unicode` string (in Python 2, which is the same as a `str` string in Python 3), you can use the `tounicode` function from the fonttools `py23` module, which will just pass it on as is if it's already a unicode string, or decode the bytes string to a unicode string using the provided encoding (it defaults to `ascii` but you should provide the actual bytes string encoding; if you read the bytes from a text file, that would usually be UTF-8, if you read them from the console (in Python 2) it will be the console's encoding, etc.).

If you want to work with strings in a codebase that's meant to support both python 2 and 3, I'm afraid you need to become familiar with terms like bytes, unicode, str, encode, decode, etc.
In an ideal world where everybody is using python 3, then one would just use `str` for text, and `bytes` for binary data, without confusing one with the other like python 2 used to do. ;)

We should discuss this tomorrow in the meeting (though I don't think @milliams will be there). I think that we need to decide on the specifics of the `get/put` API. I personally think that this should be defined as:

`get` - get file from remoteDir to localDir
`put` - put the file from localDir to remoteDir
`copy_to` - copy a file from wherever seems best to the given directory. Would make use of 'get' code and probably just needs to be implemented in `IGangaFile` as is generic for all.

`LocalFile` can be made to fit in with this scheme if you call the Ganga Repository the `remoteDir`.

I believe this covers all the functionality that's needed. Now there is a debate as to whether you implement the `copy_to` function as a separate function or as an additional argument in the `get` method. I would argue for a separate function as it won't muddy the waters of `get` and you could also make it generic code in the base class, as you would just do something like:
```
Is file in localdir?
   Yes -> cp from here
   No -> call get function and **then** cp from localdir
```

But as I say, this should be discussed with more people as this `IGangaFile` stuff is not well defined at present and so we don't want to make decisions now that could hurt us in the future.

The sync cable and the commands for whether a camera is space or master is all that is needed to enable HW sync. You can do this from different PCs. But of course sync cable DOES need to connect cameras. You can make a wireless version yourself if you have a longer range as it is simply a sync pulse that needs to be shared. 
However you will need to properly collect frames together using the frame counters (which will be offset for each camera but in sync). 

When you set a camera to slave it will still acquire frames at the right frame rate even if there is no sync input signal. It tries to sync but if there is no signal that comes in at the right time it gives up and triggers itself.

Seeing `... USB2` in the Viewer almost always indicate a setup problem. This is not something we have great deal of control over - the OS is the one who will ask for either USB3 or USB2 descriptors from the camera; once enumerated as USB2, the camera will not get sufficient bandwidth from the OS to offer both color and depth (it is possible that in future versions of firmware we will add some sort of low-res depth+color mode, but right now it is not supported)
Please try changing the port (as @gjmsoftware) suggested and consider trying a different cable.

The calibration can correct the deviation/rotation of the camera sensor position. Each D415/D435 has the factory calibration to calibrate the intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. The dynamic calibration for end users can help calibrate the extrinsic parameters like translation and rotation between two cameras in D415/D435.
The maximum use range depends on test scene, light condition, IR laser power, etc. Indeed, the depth accuracy will be impacted/changed if a far distance used. More texture on the target surface can help the depth. Or, IR pattern (from IR projector) can help the depth.

* with ansible I generally agree, no python 3 support and even simple things (ie. getting command's `stdout`) are quite difficult to achieve through api + neccessity of requiring more config files (inventory, ansible.cfg)
* local vs. remote commands: we should take into account also multinode testing (is there even need for it, @bmbouter ?). If there will be multiple nodes specified in config file, we might want to set it differently for each machine. So in `settings.json` file we could have named nodes with it's hostname/ip and process would be executed based on it's value (locally or over ssh). 1. A sentence/document vector is obtained by averaging the word/ngram embeddings.
2. For the classification task, [multinomial logistic regression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multinomial_logistic_regression) is used, where the sentence/document vector corresponds to the features. When applying fastText on problems with a large number of classes, you can use the hierarchical softmax to speed-up the computation (with the command line option `-loss hs`).

Please note that the two weight matrices (corresponding to the word/ngram embeddings and the classifiers) are learned jointly.

Best,
Edouard.

Hi @luosmart,
When training models with a large number of labels, the hierarchical softmax should be used, using the command line option `-loss hs`:
```
./fasttext supervised -input "${DATADIR}/label_user_session_shuf_train" -output "${RESULTDIR}/session" -dim 50 -lr 0.1 -epoch 2 -thread 6 -loss hs
```
You can also try to add ngram features, using the command line option `-wordNgrams 2`.

> I have some trouble seeing why that would guarantee the guards aren't destroyed until the call is finished; wouldn't it be valid for a compiler to destroy them after evaluating the initializer list?

Temporary objects are cleaned up at the end of the full expression in which they appear -- essentially at `;`. This is what makes nested and chained calls work in C++ (reference lifetime extension doesn't propagate through function returns).

That being said, the init list is still ugly and I don't like. Your `call_guardian` idea is much better!

The only question I have now: `call_guard<T1, T2>` or `call_guard<T1>, call_guard<T2>`? The former is trivial to implement and it would make a terser syntax. The latter is more versatile since additional guards can be added at a later stage, but it does require passing `Extra...` through a filter metafunction. I assume the more versatile one is more desirable?

I think it would be nice to avoid shadowing builtin names like `object` and `type`. While they are formally in the `__builtin__` (2.7) / `builtins` (3.x) modules, the objects are always available unqualified globally. I don't think that shadowing them would be too much of a technical issue, but perhaps confusing upon user inspection, especially as Python 2.7 uses unqualified names. E.g. looking up `type(A).__name__` for a pybind11 class and seeing `type` just like a regular Python class may lead someone to the wrong conclusion while debugging. Similarly for bases and `object`. 

That said, I'm not attached to the `pybind11_`-prefix-everywhere naming scheme and don't mind a bit of bikeshedding on this, just without shadowing the builtins. Note that `__module__ == ""` is a possibility, I just  didn't go for it here because `pydoc` leaves the dot prefix anyway.

Happy to have a separate meeting or just chat here, but I'm unsure we want to add this feature. I have some concerns about how much this really buys users when it works, the additional code complexity, and the additional cognitive complexity pushed on users trying to understand this feature, but my biggest concerns are:

#### Do we want users to click through TLS warnings?

The main motivation I heard for this feature and the reason we need it now when we didn't for HTTP->HTTPS redirects is that users can't click through TLS warnings on sites that used HSTS. Is the fact that we disable HSTS so users can do this beneficial? I know many users do click through these warnings when they can, but most should not and browsers are trying all the time to reduce the rate that users do so.

Even more seriously with us talking about making this feature Certbot's default behavior some day, do all sysadmins want their users to (be able to) send their data to a potentially compromised site or should they wait for things to be fixed? I think that depends on the Certbot user and the site and us making this change allows users to make more harmful decisions by default. Perhaps we could make this aspect of AutoHSTS configurable, but that brings me back to my concerns about code and cognitive complexity.

#### We can't disable HSTS reliably

There are many cases where both renewal will fail and we'll be unable to disable HSTS.  A few examples of this are:

* certbot-auto upgraded and failed to install the new version of Certbot. See #2902 or #2883 for a couple examples of this.
* Certbot's Python environment became corrupted. This can occur both with `certbot-auto` and OS packages. See #2544, #2823, or #5104.
* We cannot parse the user's Apache config. See #4025.
* We could not find the identifier placed in the vhost with AutoHSTS so we could disable it.
* We cannot parse the renewal configuration file.

Neither us or our users can rely on this feature to work. I think features like this can lull us and our users into a false sense of security and leave everyone in a bad place when it doesn't work as expected. I'd rather we worked under the assumption that we cannot do this and focus on improving documentation, features, and usability in ways we expect to be reliable.

We are talking about two levels of parallelisms: DAG level and step level. At the step level, currently only tasks can be run in parallel as external tasks. At the DAG level, independent paths will be executed in parallel, and the steps **will wait for the completion of tasks, regardless of external or internal**.

However, one  of the motivations for the current implementation is that sos currently uses workers for both step and tasks, so `-j8` will have at most 8 workers for either step or task (steps waiting for tasks do not count). In the internal model of parallelism, sos does not know how many processes are used in each step so at most 64 workers would be used if 8 steps each running 8 internal tasks....

Hi @JunhongXu,  we are using a custom serializer for regular (non-cuda) pytorch tensors defined at https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/1ee7d11f562210474921ba07f591fd2df800c757/python/pyarrow/serialization.py#L102-L124.

Can you try defining a custom serializer, e.g.,

```python
import ray
import torch

ray.init()

x = torch.cuda.FloatTensor(5, 5).random_()

def serializer(obj):
    return obj.cpu().numpy()

def deserializer(serialized_obj):
    return torch.from_numpy(serialized_obj).cuda()

ray.register_custom_serializer(torch.cuda.FloatTensor, serializer=serializer, deserializer=deserializer)

# Now the following should work!
x_id = ray.put(x)
y = ray.get(x_id)
```

If that works for you then we should probably make that the default in pyarrow.

Note that this unfortunately goes through CPU memory.. we're slowly working on GPU support for the object store, but it isn't ready yet.

It's been a while since I thought about this. Looking at it again, I really agree with https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1031#issuecomment-271276948 above - I don't think the base Widget class is the right place to have a list of default attributes. Those attributes are pretty specific to our bundled widgets, while Widget is used to build many other types of widgets in third party packages (e.g., bqplot, pythreejs, ipyvolume, etc.).

In an interactive system, printing out the entire state of the widget also is onerous.

So to move forward, we could (a) make the __repr__ depend on the widget type (i.e., the slider widget would have a nice repr with some of the values in the widget, which would be different than the values in the text widget, or the tab widget, for example).

OR for the purposes of doctesting, we could just print the result of the `get_state()` method. It wouldn't recurse into children widgets, but would be a start.

Registering it in a Django AppConfig (or anywhere else in the standard Django import path) is problematic because of #121. I chose to build a custom management command for the scheduler and do the setup there.

```python
import datetime
import logging

import django_rq
from django_rq.management.commands import rqscheduler

scheduler = django_rq.get_scheduler()
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def clear_scheduled_jobs():
    # Delete any existing jobs in the scheduler when the app starts up
    for job in scheduler.get_jobs():
        log.debug("Deleting scheduled job %s", job)
        job.delete()

def register_scheduled_jobs():
    # do your scheduling here
    scheduler.schedule(...)


class Command(rqscheduler.Command):
    def handle(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # This is necessary to prevent dupes
        clear_scheduled_jobs()
        register_scheduled_jobs()
        super(Command, self).handle(*args, **kwargs)
```
